frameworks.name: Bayesian observer with efficient coding
frameworks.one_line: perception as Bayesian inference constrained by stimulus-prior-shaped encoding accuracy

frameworks.name: categorical loss / natural orientation categories
frameworks.one_line: adds discrete category loss (horizontal/vertical/CW/CCW) on top of squared-error loss

frameworks.name: Bayesian observer without efficient coding
frameworks.one_line: uniform-encoding Bayesian model used as model-comparison baseline

core_question: Are both efficient coding and categorical loss necessary to quantitatively account for human orientation estimation biases?
key_assumptions: encoding accuracy reflects the natural prior over local orientations
observers minimize a weighted mix of squared-error and categorical loss
four natural orientation categories defined relative to a noisy reference
manipulations: presence vs absence of efficient coding in the observer model
presence vs absence of a categorical loss term
sensory noise level driving bias magnitude
key_findings: models without efficient coding fail to capture direction, magnitude, and noise-dependence of bias
efficient-coding models reproduce repulsive bias away from cardinals
efficient coding alone mispredicts the variance pattern of estimates
only efficient coding plus categorical loss fits the full estimate distribution
interpretations: both efficient coding and categorical loss are necessary for orientation perception
categorical structure contributes to perceptual distortions beyond prior-based encoding
